subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A surgeon performs a routine appendicectomy but inadvertently ligates the ureter, leading to the patient's death. Under the CURRENT LAW (BNS 2024), which section governs criminal liability for causing death by medical negligence?
explanation: BNS Section 106 replaced IPC Section 304A and governs causing death by rash or negligent act. For medical professionals, it carries a punishment of up to 5 years imprisonment and/or fine. BNS 103 is murder, BNS 101 is culpable homicide not amounting to murder, and BNS 125 is rash/negligent act endangering life without causing death.
highYield: BNS Section 106 (formerly IPC 304A) — medical negligence causing death; max 5 years + fine
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Under BNS 2024, which section defines rape and specifies the 7 circumstances under which sexual intercourse constitutes rape, with the age of consent remaining at 18 years?
explanation: BNS Section 63 defines rape (formerly IPC Section 375), enumerating 7 circumstances of non-consent including without will, against will, with fraud, with intoxication, and with a minor under 18 years. BNS 64 prescribes punishment for rape, BNS 70 covers gang rape, and BNS 75 covers sexual harassment.
highYield: BNS Section 63 (formerly IPC 375) — definition of rape; 7 circumstances; age of consent = 18 years
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 20-year-old woman is assaulted by a group of four men. Under BNS 2024, which section covers gang rape and what is the minimum sentence?
explanation: BNS Section 70 (formerly IPC 376D) specifically addresses gang rape, defined as rape committed by one or more persons constituting a group. The minimum punishment is 20 years rigorous imprisonment, extendable to life imprisonment. BNS 64 covers individual rape with a minimum of 10 years. BNS 75 governs sexual harassment, not gang rape.
highYield: BNS Section 70 (formerly IPC 376D) — gang rape; minimum 20 years to life imprisonment
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Under BNS 2024, which section lists the 8 types of grievous hurt, previously enumerated under IPC Section 320?
explanation: BNS Section 114 (formerly IPC 320) enumerates the 8 types of grievous hurt: emasculation, permanent loss of sight, permanent loss of hearing, loss of any member or joint, destruction of any member or joint, permanent disfiguration of head/face, fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth, and any hurt endangering life or causing severe bodily pain lasting 20 days. BNS 115 is voluntarily causing hurt and BNS 117 is voluntarily causing grievous hurt.
highYield: BNS Section 114 (formerly IPC 320) — 8 types of grievous hurt; same list retained under BNS
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man with severe schizophrenia kills his neighbour, genuinely believing the neighbour was a demon sent to destroy the world. Under BNS 2024, which section provides the defence of unsoundness of mind (McNaughton rules)?
explanation: BNS Section 22 (formerly IPC Section 84) incorporates the McNaughton rules, providing complete defence for acts committed by a person of unsound mind who, at the time of the act, did not know the nature of the act or did not know it was wrong. BNS 19 covers absolute immunity for children under 7 years, BNS 20 covers children 7-12 years, and BNS 24 covers intoxication defences.
highYield: BNS Section 22 (formerly IPC 84) — unsoundness of mind; McNaughton rules; complete defence
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 5-year-old child pushes a classmate off a balcony, causing death. Under BNS 2024, which section provides absolute criminal immunity to this child?
explanation: BNS Section 19 (formerly IPC Section 82) provides absolute immunity to children under 7 years of age — nothing is an offence done by a child under 7 years. This is doli incapax — incapable of crime by age. BNS 20 covers children 7-12 years who may be held responsible if they have reached sufficient maturity of understanding. BNS 22 covers unsoundness of mind.
highYield: BNS Section 19 (formerly IPC 82) — absolute immunity for children under 7 years; doli incapax
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A burn victim in the emergency department is in critical condition. A police constable records her statement as she describes her attacker. Under BNSS 2024, which section governs the recording of dying declaration?
explanation: BNSS Section 183 (formerly CrPC Section 164) governs the recording of statements and confessions, including dying declarations. A magistrate is preferred for recording a dying declaration, but any witness is legally acceptable if a magistrate is unavailable. BNSS Section 194 covers the inquest procedure. CrPC 164 and CrPC 174 are the old provisions now replaced.
highYield: BNSS Section 183 (formerly CrPC 164) — dying declaration; magistrate preferred; any witness acceptable
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A body is found under suspicious circumstances in a railway yard. Under BNSS 2024, which section mandates the police inquest procedure to determine the cause and manner of death?
explanation: BNSS Section 194 (formerly CrPC Section 174) governs the police inquest procedure for unnatural or suspicious deaths. It mandates the officer-in-charge to investigate the apparent cause of death, prepare an inquest report, and send the body for postmortem if necessary. BNSS 183 governs dying declarations and statements. CrPC 174 is the old provision now superseded.
highYield: BNSS Section 194 (formerly CrPC 174) — police inquest; unnatural/suspicious deaths
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man plans and executes the killing of his business rival with a firearm. Under BNS 2024, which section defines and punishes murder?
explanation: BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC Section 302) defines and punishes murder with death penalty or life imprisonment, along with fine. Murder requires intention to kill or knowledge that the act is imminently dangerous and likely to cause death. BNS 101 is culpable homicide not amounting to murder, BNS 106 is causing death by negligence, and BNS 115 is voluntarily causing hurt.
highYield: BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC 302) — murder; punishment is death or life imprisonment
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man in a sudden quarrel strikes his opponent with a wooden plank intending to cause grievous hurt; the opponent dies unexpectedly. Under BNS 2024, which section most appropriately governs this act of culpable homicide not amounting to murder?
explanation: BNS Section 101 (formerly IPC Sections 299/304) covers culpable homicide not amounting to murder. When the act is done with intention to cause such bodily injury as is likely to cause death but without intention to cause death, and without knowledge that it is imminently dangerous, it falls under BNS 101. This is distinct from murder (BNS 103) which requires intention to kill.
highYield: BNS Section 101 (formerly IPC 299/304) — culpable homicide not amounting to murder
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: Medical Negligence — BNS vs IPC trap
stem: A patient dies following a doctor's failure to administer prescribed anticoagulants post-cardiac surgery. A NEET PG student in 2025 is asked which section of CURRENT LAW applies to this medical negligence causing death. The correct answer is:
options: IPC Section 304A — as it is the established provision for negligent death
BNS Section 106 — as BNS replaced IPC from July 1, 2024
BNS Section 103 — as the death was foreseeable
BNS Section 101 — as there was some degree of intent
explanation: Since July 1, 2024, the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2024 replaced the Indian Penal Code. IPC Section 304A is now replaced by BNS Section 106, which governs causing death by rash or negligent act. Students who memorised IPC 304A will be caught by this trap in 2025 NEET PG. The current operative law mandates citing BNS 106, not IPC 304A.
highYield: BNS Section 106 is the CURRENT law for medical negligence causing death — IPC 304A is repealed as of July 1, 2024
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A hospital staff member installs a hidden camera in the changing room used by female patients. Under BNS 2024, which section applies to this act of voyeurism?
explanation: BNS Section 77 (formerly IPC Section 354C) defines and punishes voyeurism — watching or capturing images of a woman engaging in a private act without her consent. In a healthcare setting this has severe professional and criminal consequences. BNS 75 covers sexual harassment, BNS 63 defines rape, and BNS 64 punishes rape.
highYield: BNS Section 77 (formerly IPC 354C) — voyeurism; hidden cameras/watching private acts without consent
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A male colleague repeatedly makes unwanted sexual comments to a female colleague at a medical college and also shows her pornographic content on his phone. Under BNS 2024, which section covers sexual harassment?
explanation: BNS Section 75 (formerly IPC Section 354A) covers sexual harassment, including unwelcome physical contact, demands for sexual favours, sexually coloured remarks, and showing pornography without consent. The POSH Act (Prevention of Sexual Harassment at Workplace, 2013) may also apply in a workplace context but BNS 75 is the criminal provision.
highYield: BNS Section 75 (formerly IPC 354A) — sexual harassment; 4 defined acts including showing pornography
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A doctor in a government hospital examines a 14-year-old girl who presents with evidence of sexual assault. Regarding mandatory reporting, which of the following is correct under current law?
options: The doctor must report under BNS Section 63 to the nearest police station
The doctor must report under POCSO Act Section 19 to the Special Juvenile Police Unit or local police
Reporting is optional if parents are present and decline to report
The doctor must report to the Child Welfare Committee within 48 hours under BNSS
explanation: POCSO Act 2012 (Protection of Children from Sexual Offences) is a separate legislation from IPC/BNS and its sections are UNCHANGED. Under POCSO Section 19, any person including a doctor who has knowledge of a sexual offence committed against a child (under 18 years) MUST report it to the Special Juvenile Police Unit or local police. Non-reporting is itself a criminal offence. BNS does not override POCSO obligations.
highYield: POCSO Act Section 19 — mandatory reporting by any person including doctors; POCSO sections UNCHANGED by BNS
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with evidence of anal penetration. Under the POCSO Act 2012, which section covers penetrative sexual assault and what is the minimum punishment?
options: POCSO Section 4; minimum 10 years rigorous imprisonment
POCSO Section 6; minimum 20 years rigorous imprisonment
explanation: POCSO Section 4 covers penetrative sexual assault on a child (under 18 years) and prescribes a minimum of 10 years rigorous imprisonment extendable to life, plus fine. POCSO Section 6 covers aggravated penetrative sexual assault (minimum 20 years to life). POCSO sections are completely separate from BNS and remain unchanged. BNS sections do not apply when POCSO is applicable.
highYield: POCSO Section 4 — penetrative sexual assault; minimum 10 years; POCSO is separate from IPC/BNS
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric ward. Examination reveals evidence of sexual assault committed by her father. Under POCSO Act 2012, the applicable section for aggravated penetrative sexual assault by a relative is:
options: POCSO Section 6 — aggravated penetrative sexual assault
explanation: POCSO Section 6 covers aggravated penetrative sexual assault, which includes assault by a relative, guardian, teacher, police officer, or public servant. The minimum punishment is 20 years rigorous imprisonment extendable to life, or death in the most heinous cases. This is distinct from Section 4 (basic penetrative assault, 10 years minimum). BNS does not apply when POCSO covers the case.
highYield: POCSO Section 6 — aggravated penetrative sexual assault by relative/authority figure; minimum 20 years
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 12-year-old girl reports that a neighbour touched her private parts without penetration. The doctor examining her should be aware that under POCSO Act 2012, which sections cover sexual assault (non-penetrative) and the related offence?
options: POCSO Section 7 and Section 8 — sexual assault and its punishment
BNS Section 63 and Section 64 — rape definition and punishment
explanation: POCSO Section 7 defines sexual assault (non-penetrative) as touching the sexual organ, anus, or breast of a child with sexual intent. Section 8 prescribes the punishment: minimum 3 years, maximum 5 years rigorous imprisonment with fine. These are separate from penetrative assault sections (4 and 6). The POCSO Act is entirely independent of BNS and IPC.
highYield: POCSO Section 7 — sexual assault (non-penetrative); Section 8 — punishment; 3-5 years RI
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A junior resident drives at high speed in a hospital campus and injures a pedestrian but the pedestrian survives. Under BNS 2024, the rash or negligent act endangering life but NOT causing death is governed by:
explanation: BNS Section 125 (formerly IPC Sections 336/337/338) covers rash or negligent act endangering life or personal safety of others. This section applies when injury occurs but the victim does not die. When death occurs due to negligence, BNS Section 106 applies. BNS 101 covers culpable homicide and BNS 115 covers voluntarily causing hurt.
highYield: BNS Section 125 (formerly IPC 336/337/338) — rash/negligent act endangering life without causing death
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A psychiatrist locks a patient in a room against the patient's will and without legal authorisation, preventing all escape. Under BNS 2024, this act constitutes:
options: BNS Section 22 — unsoundness of mind defence
explanation: BNS Section 127 (formerly IPC Section 340) covers wrongful confinement — wrongfully restraining a person in such a manner as to prevent them from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits. Locking someone in a room constitutes confinement. BNS Section 126 (formerly IPC 339) covers wrongful restraint, which is a lesser degree — preventing movement in a particular direction without complete enclosure.
highYield: BNS Section 127 (formerly IPC 340) — wrongful confinement; complete prevention from leaving a defined space
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A person consumes alcohol voluntarily and in an intoxicated state commits murder. He pleads that he did not know the nature of his act due to intoxication. Under BNS 2024, the intoxication defence is governed by which section, and is it a valid complete defence here?
options: BNS Section 23; valid complete defence if intoxication was voluntary
BNS Section 23/24; NOT a complete defence if intoxication was voluntary and self-induced
BNS Section 22; valid — same as unsoundness of mind
BNS Section 19; valid — absolute immunity applies
explanation: BNS Sections 23 and 24 (formerly IPC 85/86) govern the intoxication defence. When intoxication is involuntary (administered without knowledge/consent), it can provide a complete defence. However, when intoxication is VOLUNTARY and self-induced, it is NOT a complete defence. The person is presumed to have the same intention as if they were sober. This differs from insanity (BNS 22) which can provide a complete defence.
highYield: BNS Section 23/24 (formerly IPC 85/86) — voluntary intoxication is NOT a complete defence; involuntary intoxication may be
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: BNS vs IPC — Rape Punishment Comparison
stem: A medical student is comparing the old IPC provisions with the current BNS 2024. Regarding punishment for rape committed by a man on a woman above 18 years, which statement CORRECTLY reflects the change from IPC to BNS?
options: IPC 376 prescribed minimum 7 years; BNS 64 now prescribes minimum 5 years — sentence reduced
IPC 376 prescribed minimum 7 years; BNS 64 now prescribes minimum 10 years — sentence increased
Both IPC 376 and BNS 64 prescribe the same minimum of 7 years — no change
BNS 64 applies only to gang rape; individual rape is now under BNS 63
explanation: Under the old IPC Section 376, the minimum punishment for rape was 7 years rigorous imprisonment. Under BNS Section 64 (replacing IPC 376), the minimum sentence has been INCREASED to 10 years rigorous imprisonment, extendable to life imprisonment. This is a significant change and a common exam trap. BNS 63 defines rape (does not prescribe punishment), and BNS 70 covers gang rape.
highYield: BNS Section 64 — punishment for rape increased to minimum 10 years (was 7 years under IPC 376)
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A forensic expert is asked to advise a court under CURRENT LAW (2025). A person convicted of murder under BNS 2024 would be charged under BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC 302). The punishment prescribed is:
options: Death penalty or life imprisonment with fine — same as IPC 302
Life imprisonment only; death penalty abolished under BNS
explanation: BNS Section 103 retains the same punishment as the former IPC Section 302 — death penalty or life imprisonment, along with fine. The BNS did not abolish capital punishment for murder. The section number changed (302 to 103) but the substance of punishment remained the same. Life imprisonment without possibility of death penalty applies only to specific BNS subsections.
highYield: BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC 302) — murder; punishment unchanged: death or life imprisonment + fine
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: In a rape case, the forensic pathologist collects DNA evidence from the victim. Under the current legal framework (2025), the admissibility of DNA evidence in Indian courts is governed by:
options: Indian Evidence Act, Section 45 — expert opinion
explanation: The Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA) 2023 replaced the Indian Evidence Act 1872 and governs admissibility of all forms of evidence in Indian courts, including DNA evidence. BSA explicitly recognises electronic/digital evidence and forensic evidence. The old Indian Evidence Act Section 45 (expert opinion) is now subsumed under BSA provisions. BNSS 183 covers dying declarations and BNS 63 defines rape.
highYield: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA) 2023 — replaced Indian Evidence Act; governs DNA evidence admissibility
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 9-year-old boy sets fire to a neighbour's house after being teased. He claims he did not understand that people inside could be harmed. Under BNS 2024, his criminal liability depends on:
options: Whether he had reached sufficient maturity of understanding at the time — BNS Section 20
BNS Section 22 — whether he was of unsound mind
Automatic liability as he is above 7 years — BNS Section 103
explanation: BNS Section 20 (formerly IPC Section 83) applies to children between 7 and 12 years. Unlike children under 7 who have absolute immunity (BNS 19), a child aged 7-12 may be held criminally responsible IF it is proved that at the time of the act, they had attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of the act. The burden is on the prosecution to prove this maturity.
highYield: BNS Section 20 (formerly IPC 83) — children 7-12 years; liability depends on maturity of understanding
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A paediatrician examines a 16-year-old boy who discloses sexual abuse by a teacher. The parents beg the doctor not to report it to avoid social stigma. Under current law, which statement is CORRECT?
options: The doctor may withhold reporting if parents give written consent to not report
Reporting is mandatory under POCSO Section 19 regardless of parental consent; failure to report is a criminal offence
The doctor must report under BNS Section 75 as it replaces POCSO reporting obligations
Reporting is required only if the victim is female, as POCSO applies only to girls
explanation: POCSO Section 19 mandates that any person who has apprehension or knowledge of a sexual offence against a child MUST report it to the Special Juvenile Police Unit (SJPU) or local police. This obligation is absolute and cannot be waived by parental consent. Failure to report is a criminal offence punishable under POCSO Section 21. POCSO applies to children of all genders (below 18 years). BNS does NOT replace POCSO reporting obligations.
highYield: POCSO Section 19 — mandatory reporting by ANY person including doctors; parental consent irrelevant; failure = criminal offence (Section 21)
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A senior resident slaps a junior resident during rounds, causing a minor bruise. Under BNS 2024, this act of voluntarily causing hurt is covered under which section?
explanation: BNS Section 115 (formerly IPC Sections 321/323) covers the act of voluntarily causing hurt — bodily pain, disease, or infirmity caused intentionally. A minor bruise from a slap constitutes 'hurt' but not 'grievous hurt.' BNS Section 117 is voluntarily causing grievous hurt (for the 8 types listed in BNS 114). BNS 114 lists the types of grievous hurt. BNS 127 is wrongful confinement.
highYield: BNS Section 115 (formerly IPC 321/323) — voluntarily causing hurt; not grievous; minor bodily pain/injury
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man deliberately throws acid on a colleague's face, causing permanent disfigurement. Under BNS 2024, which section covers the act of voluntarily causing grievous hurt?
explanation: BNS Section 117 (formerly IPC Sections 322/325) covers voluntarily causing grievous hurt. Permanent disfigurement of the face is one of the 8 types of grievous hurt enumerated in BNS Section 114. When grievous hurt is caused voluntarily, BNS 117 applies. For acid attacks specifically, there are also enhanced punishment provisions. BNS 115 covers ordinary hurt and BNS 114 only defines the types.
highYield: BNS Section 117 (formerly IPC 322/325) — voluntarily causing grievous hurt; permanent facial disfigurement qualifies
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: BNS vs IPC — Rape Definition Trap
stem: A medical student studying for NEET PG 2025 is asked: 'The definition of rape and the 7 circumstances of non-consent, previously under IPC Section 375, is now found under which section of current law?' The correct answer is:
options: IPC Section 375 — still the operative provision as it has not been repealed
BNS Section 64 — punishment section which also contains the definition
BNS Section 63 — the definition of rape under current law
BNSS Section 183 — as rape must be reported under the procedural code
explanation: Since July 1, 2024, IPC Section 375 has been REPEALED and replaced by BNS Section 63, which contains the complete definition of rape including all 7 circumstances. BNS Section 64 contains only the punishment provisions for rape (minimum 10 years). Students who cite IPC 375 in 2025 NEET PG will lose marks. BNSS 183 is a procedural provision for statements/declarations.
highYield: BNS Section 63 (NOT IPC 375 — repealed July 1, 2024) — definition of rape; 7 circumstances of non-consent
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: Medical Negligence — Rash vs Negligent Act
stem: An anaesthesiologist fails to monitor a patient's oxygen saturation during surgery; the patient develops hypoxic brain injury but survives. The hospital administration wants to know which section of BNS 2024 applies to this medical negligence where there is NO death:
options: BNS Section 106 — causing death by negligence
BNS Section 125 — rash or negligent act endangering life
BNS Section 101 — culpable homicide not amounting to murder
explanation: When a negligent act endangers life or causes bodily injury but does NOT result in death, BNS Section 125 (formerly IPC 336/337/338) applies. BNS 106 (formerly IPC 304A) requires actual death as a consequence. Since the patient survived, BNS 125 is the applicable provision. This is an important distinction in medical negligence cases — the outcome (death vs. injury) determines which section applies.
highYield: BNS 125 — negligent act causing injury without death; BNS 106 — only when death results; outcome determines section
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old road accident victim in the ICU shows absence of all brainstem reflexes, positive apnoea test, and two confirmatory assessments 6 hours apart by independent physicians. Brain death is certified. Organ harvesting proceeds. The legal framework governing brain death certification and organ transplantation in India is:
options: BNS Section 106 — medical negligence if transplant fails
Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act (THOTA) 1994, amended 2023
BNSS Section 194 — inquest must be conducted before organ retrieval
POCSO Act if the victim is under 18 years
explanation: Brain death certification and organ transplantation in India are governed by the Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act (THOTA) 1994, most recently amended in 2023. The 2023 amendment recognized Donation after Cardiac Death (DCD). Brain death definition under THOTA requires absence of all brainstem reflexes, positive apnoea test, and two physician certifications 6 hours apart (Harvard 1968 criteria/AIIMS protocol). BNS 106 is for negligence causing death, not transplantation.
highYield: THOTA 1994 (amended 2023) — brain death certification; 2023 amendment added DCD recognition; 2 physicians 6 hours apart
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: Wrongful Restraint vs Wrongful Confinement — BNS 2024
stem: A doctor refuses to allow a patient to leave the hospital, posting security guards at every exit. The patient is free to move within the ward but cannot leave. Under BNS 2024, this scenario BEST fits:
options: BNS Section 126 — wrongful restraint, as the patient can still move within the ward
BNS Section 127 — wrongful confinement, as circumscribing limits are imposed
explanation: BNS Section 127 (formerly IPC 340) covers wrongful confinement — restraining a person within circumscribing limits (such as a ward or hospital). The patient cannot leave the hospital, which constitutes confinement within defined boundaries. BNS Section 126 (wrongful restraint, formerly IPC 339) prevents movement in a particular direction but does not involve circumscribing limits. The key distinction: confinement = enclosed area; restraint = directional prevention.
highYield: BNS 127 = wrongful confinement (circumscribing limits); BNS 126 = wrongful restraint (directional); critical distinction
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG
